landing.title: Verified Identity. On-chain.
landing.lede: Mint your Verified Ukrainian certificate. Your identity stays on your machine — only the proof reaches the chain.
landing.subline: Powered by Diia QES + Groth16. Your identity bytes never enter this browser.
landing.ceremonyLink: Help with the trusted setup ceremony →

privacy.heading: Identity, escrowed.
privacy.onLedgerBody: a nullifier — context-bound, one-way, unlinkable across applications.
privacy.notOnLedgerLabel: What is not on the ledger
privacy.notOnLedgerBody: name, address, document numbers, signature, certificate contents.
privacy.recoveryLabel: What can be recovered, by whom, under what process
privacy.recoveryBody: by the issuing authority, under lawful order, at meaningful compute cost. Not by third parties.
privacy.closing: This is identity escrow. Every-day pseudonymity for the holder; recoverable accountability for the state. The same trust structure as the qualified electronic signature itself — preserved on-chain.

cli.title: Install zkqes CLI for native proof generation.
cli.lede: Optional. The browser prover works as-is. Install the CLI to make proof generation about 7× faster and 10× lighter on memory.
cli.whyFaster: About 14 s native rapidsnark vs ~90 s in-browser snarkjs on the same machine.
cli.whyLighter: ≈3.7 GB peak native vs ≈38 GB in-browser. Phones / low-RAM laptops can finally generate proofs.
cli.whyOnlyOnInvoke: Not a daemon. You start it with `zkqes serve`, leave it running while you generate proofs, then Ctrl+C when done.
cli.whyPrivate: The CLI binds to localhost:9080. The browser fetches it via the same-origin pin (https://app.zkqes.org); no other origin can talk to it.
cli.installLede: V1 ships via npm. Requires Node 20 or newer; works on macOS, Linux, and Windows + WSL.
cli.installNpmNote: Postinstall downloads the rapidsnark sidecar matching your OS + arch (~12 MB). The CLI itself is a ~32 KB tarball.
cli.windowsCalloutBody: rapidsnark has no Windows prebuilt in V1. Native Windows users must build rapidsnark themselves and pass `zkqes serve --rapidsnark-bin C:\\path\\to\\rapidsnark.exe`. WSL users install rapidsnark inside the WSL distro normally — no extra flag needed.
cli.deferredChannels: Homebrew formula and a one-line curl installer (`brew install alik-eth/homebrew-zkqes/zkqes` and `curl -fsSL https://zkqes.org/install.sh | sh`) are coming in V1.1. For V1, npm is the supported channel.
cli.runBody: Open a terminal and start the server. Leave it running while you generate proofs — it binds to localhost:9080 and accepts /prove POSTs from https://app.zkqes.org only.
cli.runStop: Stop with Ctrl+C when finished. The server does not auto-start; nothing runs in the background.
cli.verifyBody: With zkqes serve running, go back to the register flow. The "Install zkqes" banner disappears when the browser detects the running server, and your prove step shows "proved via: cli" instead of "browser".
cli.troublePortBody: Another process is bound to :9080. Either stop it (`lsof -i :9080`, then kill the PID) or pass `zkqes serve --port <other>`. The browser only auto-detects :9080 in V1; alternate ports work but the banner won't auto-disappear.
cli.troubleSidecarBody: If postinstall couldn't download the sidecar (offline machine, restricted CI, or native Windows where iden3 v0.0.8 ships no prebuilt), pass `zkqes serve --rapidsnark-bin <path>` with a binary you built yourself. Run `zkqes cache rebuild` while online to retry the postinstall download on platforms that have a prebuilt.
cli.troubleManifestBody: On first run the CLI fetches the V5.2 zkey manifest from zkqes.org. If your network blocks it, pass `--manifest-url file:///path/to/local-manifest.json` with a vendored copy. The CLI verifies the manifest signature against the embedded public key in either case.

submit.title: Submit your proof
submit.lede: Drop the proof.json the CLI generated. We submit it to the registry on-chain.
submit.drop: Drag proof.json here, or click to browse

mint.title: Mint your certificate
mint.connectPrompt: Connect a wallet to mint your certificate.

cliBanner.title: Faster proofs with the zkqes CLI
cliBanner.body: Install zkqes for native proof generation — about 7× faster (≈14 s vs ≈90 s) and 10× less memory (≈3.7 GB vs ≈38 GB) than the in-browser prover. Optional — the browser prover works as-is.

registerV5.title: Register your identity
registerV5.lede: Four steps. Your private credentials never leave this browser — only a zero-knowledge proof reaches the chain.

step1.title: Connect your wallet
step1.body: The wallet you connect here will own the Verified Ukrainian certificate on-chain. Use a wallet on Base mainnet — this binding is what your Diia signature certifies.

step2.title: Generate your binding
step2.recovering: Awaiting wallet signature for pubkey recovery…
step2.downloadHint: Canonical JSON (RFC 8785). Attach this file to Diia (or your QTSP client) and produce a CAdES-BES signature over its bytes. The .p7s file you receive goes into Step 3.

step3.title: Sign with Diia
step3.body: Open the Diia client on your phone, attach the binding you just generated, and produce a CAdES-BES signature. Drop the resulting .p7s file here.
step3.drop: Drag your .p7s here, or click to browse
step3.replaceHint: Click to replace, or continue to Step 4 below.

step4.title: Prove and register
step4.ceremonyPending: Awaiting ceremony artifacts. The Phase 2 ceremony hasn't finished yet — this button activates once the prover .zkey is published.
step4.walletNotConnected: Wallet not connected. Please connect your wallet before generating a proof.
step4.scwNotYetSupported: Smart contract wallets are not yet supported for registration. Please use an EOA (MetaMask, Rabby, Frame, etc.).

mintV5.awaitingDeploy: Awaiting V5 registry deployment. Mint becomes available once orchestration §9.4 (Base Sepolia E2E) closes.

scwPassphrase.title: Smart contract wallet — passphrase required
scwPassphrase.body: Smart contract wallets cannot use the deterministic personal_sign derivation (their on-chain signature logic can change between sessions). We instead derive your identity secret from a passphrase you choose, using Argon2id.
scwPassphrase.warningTitle: 🚨 If you lose this passphrase, you cannot recover your identity
scwPassphrase.warningBody: Your passphrase is the only protection against loss of your verified identity. If you forget it, no Diia QES can recover the secret. Store it offline (paper, hardware password manager) before continuing.
scwPassphrase.deriving: Deriving identity secret via Argon2id (this takes a few seconds)…
scwPassphrase.useEoaInstead: Or: connect an EOA wallet (recommended for V5 alpha)
scwPassphrase.optedOut: SCW path cancelled — please connect an EOA wallet (MetaMask, Rabby, Frame, etc.) and retry.

accountRotate.title: Rotate to a new wallet
accountRotate.lede: Bind your verified identity to a different wallet without re-running the Diia signature flow against your old wallet. The old wallet authorises the rotation; the new wallet takes over going forward.

steps.diia: Step 2 of 6 — Upload new Diia signature
steps.derive-new: Step 3 of 6 — Sign with new wallet
steps.derive-old: Step 4 of 6 — Sign with old wallet

connect.title: Connect your new wallet
connect.body: The wallet you connect here will own the verified identity after rotation. It must be different from your current identity-bound wallet.
connect.warningBody: After rotation the old wallet can no longer prove ownership of this identity. The zkqes certificate NFT, if any, must be transferred separately via standard ERC-721 before you rotate.
connect.noWallet: No wallet connected. Please connect the wallet you want to rotate to.
connect.advance: Continue and lock this as the new wallet

diia.title: Upload your new Diia signature
diia.body: Generate a new QKB/2.0 binding pinned to your new wallet's public key, sign it via Diia, and upload the .p7s here together with the canonical binding bytes you signed.
diia.filesRequired: Both the canonical binding (.json) and the Diia signature (.p7s) must be uploaded.
diia.noPriorIdentity: No prior identity registered for this Diia certificate. There is nothing to rotate. Use /ua/registerV5 to register first.

deriveNew.title: Sign with your NEW wallet
deriveNew.body: We need a signature from your new wallet to derive its wallet-secret via HKDF. This binds the new wallet to your identity in the rotation proof.
deriveNew.walletRequired: A connected wallet is required to derive the new wallet secret.
deriveNew.wrongWallet: The currently-connected wallet does not match the locked new-wallet address. Switch back to the new wallet you locked at step 1.
deriveNew.scwNotSupported: Smart contract wallets are not yet supported for rotation. Please use an EOA wallet.

deriveOld.title: Switch to your OLD wallet and sign
deriveOld.body: Switch to your old (currently-identity-bound) wallet in your wallet extension. We need two signatures from it: one for HKDF (proves old-wallet ownership to the proof), and one over the rotation-authorisation hash (proves the old wallet consents to this exact new wallet).
deriveOld.switchHint: Switch to your old wallet in MetaMask (or other wallet extension), then click Sign.
deriveOld.stillNewWallet: Your wallet is still on the new-wallet address. Switch to your OLD wallet in the extension before signing.
deriveOld.authHashLabel: Rotation authorisation hash (keccak256 of "qkb-rotate-auth-v1" ‖ chainId ‖ registry ‖ fingerprint ‖ newWallet)
deriveOld.cta: Sign HKDF + auth hash with old wallet
deriveOld.signingHkdf: Awaiting HKDF signature from old wallet…
deriveOld.signingAuth: Awaiting auth-hash signature from old wallet…
deriveOld.walletRequired: Wallet, fingerprint, and locked new-wallet address must all be present to sign.

prove.title: Generate the rotation proof
prove.body: Building the V5.1 witness with rotation_mode=1 and producing a Groth16 proof in this browser. The proof binds the new wallet to the existing identity.
prove.ceremonyPending: Awaiting ceremony artifacts. The Phase 2 ceremony hasn't finished yet — proving stays disabled until the prover .zkey is published.
prove.missingInputs: Files, both wallet secrets, the locked new-wallet address, and the on-chain commitment read must all be present before proving.
prove.noIntCert: The .p7s does not contain an intermediate certificate. Re-export from Diia with the full chain.

submit.title: Submit the rotation transaction
submit.warningBody: Submitting will permanently rotate the identity to the new wallet. This action cannot be undone on-chain.
submit.switchBackHint: Switch back to your new wallet to submit. New wallet:
submit.switchToNew: Your wallet is still on the old-wallet address. Switch back to the NEW wallet before submitting.
submit.awaitingDeploy: Awaiting V5 registry deployment. Submission becomes available once orchestration §9.4 (Base Sepolia E2E) closes.
submit.missingData: Proof, public signals, old-wallet signature, and registry deployment must all be present to submit.

useDesktop.heading: This device can't host the zero-knowledge prover.
useDesktop.body: The prover needs about 2.5 GB of cached storage on your device — most phone browsers cap web pages well below that, so the proof would fail mid-flight. Open this page on a desktop or laptop browser instead and pick up where you left off.
useDesktop.qrCaption: Scan with another phone, or type the URL into a desktop browser.

landing.heading: A trusted setup. In public.
landing.lede: The V5 prover keys are produced by a multi-contributor ceremony. So long as one contributor honestly destroys their entropy, the ceremony is sound. We are publishing every step.
landing.whyBody: Groth16 proofs require a structured reference string. The string is generated once, by a specific party, with private randomness that — if known to anyone — would let that party forge proofs. A multi-party ceremony randomises the string across many independent contributors. Each adds entropy and destroys their own. The final string is sound under the assumption that at least one contributor was honest.
landing.publishChainBody: Every contributor's handle, round number, and signed attestation hash. Anyone can independently verify each link.
landing.publishBeaconBody: After the last contributor, we mix in a Bitcoin (or Ethereum mainnet) block hash. The block height is committed in advance — no one can predict the bytes.
landing.publishHashBody: The SHA-256 of the final prover key, committed on chain alongside the verifier contract. Anyone can verify their downloaded zkey matches.

contribute.heading: Contribute on your machine.
contribute.lede: The actual contribution runs as a local snarkjs CLI invocation on your laptop or workstation. The browser cannot host it — V8 caps WebAssembly at 4 GB of heap and the prover key needs more than that to ingest.
contribute.req32gb: snarkjs holds the intermediate zkey + working memory in V8 heap; ~30 GB peak.
contribute.reqOS: Node 20 or newer. snarkjs ≥ 0.7.4 (npm install -g snarkjs).
contribute.notSupported: Phones, tablets, and Chromebooks cannot contribute — heap caps are too low and the disk requirement exceeds typical mobile-class storage.
contribute.step1Title: Download the previous contributor's intermediate zkey
contribute.step1Body: About 2.0 GB. The download URL for your assigned round is sent at sign-up time.
contribute.step2Body: Around 20–25 minutes wall-clock on a 32 GB-RAM machine. snarkjs holds the intermediate key + working memory in V8 heap; expect ~30 GB RAM peak. Use any high-quality entropy source.
contribute.step3Body: Runs in seconds. Confirms your output zkey is structurally valid against the circuit r1cs and the Phase 1 powers-of-tau. If verify fails, do not upload.
contribute.step4Body: The signed URL is single-use, time-bounded, and tied to your assigned round. Do not share it.
contribute.signupBody: Email the admin with your handle, preferred contribution slot, and a short note on who you are. The admin assigns rounds in arrival order and replies with your download + signed-upload URLs.

flyForm.openCta: Or generate a launch command interactively →
flyForm.openHint: For contributors who would rather paste their values into a form than assemble the flyctl secrets-set line by hand.
flyForm.heading: Generate launch command
flyForm.warningBrowser: This form generates a command for you to paste into a terminal. The form runs entirely in your browser; nothing you type is sent anywhere. Your entropy stays local until you run the generated `flyctl secrets set` line.
flyForm.warningReceipt: Save the SHA-256 you'll see in `flyctl logs` — it's your contribution receipt.
flyForm.entropyError: Entropy must be exactly 64 lowercase hex characters.
flyForm.incompleteHint: (fill in all required fields above for a complete command)

status.heading: Live progress.
status.lede: Each round closes when the contributor uploads their attested intermediate zkey. We publish the chain here as it grows.
status.unavailable: Status feed unavailable. The ceremony admin publishes the JSON manually after each round; transient outages are expected. Try again in a minute.
status.plannedBlurb: The first contributor has not yet uploaded their round. Sign-ups are open.
status.completeBlurb: The final zkey is fixed. Anyone can verify their downloaded copy below.

verify.heading: Verify your zkey.
verify.lede: Drop the prover key file you downloaded. The browser computes a SHA-256 of every byte and compares against the hash committed in the on-chain verifier contract — published below once the ceremony is complete.
verify.published.pending: Ceremony is not yet complete. The final zkey hash is published here once the last contributor uploads and the public-randomness beacon is mixed in.
verify.published.feedFailed: Could not reach the ceremony status feed. The published hash will appear here once the feed is reachable; refresh the page to retry.
verify.matched: Match. This file is the same prover key the verifier contract was deployed with.
verify.mismatch: No match. Do not use this file. Re-download from the canonical source.

zkqes.headline: zk-QES.
zkqes.lede: A zero-knowledge protocol over qualified electronic signatures. Prove you hold a state-issued QES — without revealing it, the cert behind it, or the document it signed.
zkqes.framing: zkqes — the V1 implementation — is in trusted-setup ceremony. The Groth16 prover keys are produced by a multi-contributor ceremony; so long as one contributor honestly destroys their entropy, every future proof is sound. We need contributors.

pathSnarkjs.label: Local snarkjs
pathSnarkjs.body: For contributors with a 32 GB-RAM machine. Five commands, twenty-five minutes wall-clock. Snarkjs holds the intermediate zkey in V8 heap; ~30 GB peak. Bring your own entropy source.

pathVps.label: Rented VPS
pathVps.body: For contributors without local infra. A Hetzner CCX33 (32 GB, 8 vCPU, ~€0.10/hr) — or any 32 GB Linux machine — runs the same snarkjs commands. Spin up, run, attest, destroy.

pathFly.label: Fly.io launcher
pathFly.body: For everyone else. One form, your handle, your Fly token; we boot a 32 GB performance-2x machine, run snarkjs against the latest round, attest, and tear it down. Roughly $0.30/round and Fly’s free tier covers it.

zkqes.statusLink: Live ceremony status — every contributor, every round →
zkqes.umbrellaBody: zkqes is a zero-knowledge protocol over qualified electronic signatures. Any state-issued credential exhibits a property: the issuing authority retains the ability to identify a holder under lawful process. zkqes is the first protocol that surfaces this property on-chain — every-day pseudonymity for the holder, recoverable accountability for the state, the same trust structure as the qualified electronic signature itself.
